scenario: A FEEL expression must take a list of customer tags ["GOLD", "EU", "WHOLESALE"] and combine them into a single comma-separated string "GOLD, EU, WHOLESALE" -- for display in a notification.
question: Which FEEL built-in fits "join list elements into a string with a separator"?

options.text: concatenate(list) -- this function concatenates multiple lists together into a single combined list, not list elements into a string. The FEEL concatenate function operates on lists: concatenate([1,2], [3,4]) returns [1,2,3,4]. It has no string-joining capability and does not accept a separator parameter. Using concatenate for string joining produces a type error because it expects list arguments, not string lists. The correct FEEL function for joining list elements into a single delimited string is string join(list, separator) which iterates the list and concatenates elements with the separator between them.

md:549-574 (signature string join(list: list<string>, delimiter: string): string). Joins a list of strings into a single string with the delimiter between each pair. string join(["a","b","c"], ", ") returns "a, b, c" per :572-573. Items must be strings (null items are ignored per :555-557; non-string non-null items make the function return null). For ["GOLD", "EU", "WHOLESALE"] with delimiter ", ", result is "GOLD, EU, WHOLESALE".

join() method-call syntax reflex with no equivalent in FEEL. FEEL does not support method-call syntax with dot notation on values. All FEEL operations use function-call syntax with the function name first: functionName(arguments). join(separator) is the string join built-in function: string join(list, separator). For example, string join(["a", "b", "c"], ", ") returns "a, b, c". Attempting to use dot-method syntax in FEEL produces a parse error.

A manual for-expression concatenation requires careful handling of the separator placement to avoid a trailing separator, typically using index-based conditional logic: string join([for item in list return item], ", ") would still ultimately call string join. The built-in string join(list, separator) function handles the separator placement correctly and concisely without manual iteration logic.

a.text: Incorrect. string join(list, separator) is FEEL's standard list-to-string joiner. Note the FEEL function naming convention: space-separated multi-word names. The list must contain string-coercible elements (typically strings); the separator is any string. Result is the joined single string.

b.text: Correct. concatenate joins lists into a list (e.g., concatenate([1,2], [3]) = [1,2,3]). Different purpose.

c.text: Incorrect. FEEL doesn't use method-call dot syntax.

d.text: Incorrect. A manual for-loop building the string with + works but is verbose; string join is the direct primitive.

explanation: concatenate joins lists into a list (e.g., concatenate([1,2], [3]) = [1,2,3]). Different purpose.
